LESSON NOTESEch si Lëtzebuerger an ech schwätzen …In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish - Level 2, you’ll learn to introduce yourself, as well as the fact that, unlike in English, Luxembourgish normally has a different word for the nationality and the language of a country and you will review the verbs kommen, schwätzen and léieren.I will start by introducing myself:Also, mäin Numm ass Anne an ech komme vu Lëtzebuerg aus der Stad. Natierlech sinn ech Lëtzebuergerin. Ech wunnen zu Lëtzebuerg zu Hesper, an zwar um Houwald. Ech si Lëtzebuergesch Proff. So mäin Numm ass Anne is “my name is Anne”. And also means “so”. Then you may recall that to say “I come from…” is ech kommen aus …Ech kommen aus England  I come from England.Use the preposition aus with kommen. �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
RuleEch kommen aus {+ the country} and vun {+ the city} Ech kommen aus Frankräich vu Paräis.   I come from France from Paris. Exception: Use the preposition vu(n) when telling that you come from Luxembourg: Ech komme vu Lëtzebuerg. Remember here the n-rule. I And if you are born or if you grew up in Luxembourg city you add aus der Stad. So this gives us: Ech komme vu Lëtzebuerg aus der Stad. I come from Luxembourg from Luxembourg city.In my introduction I said Natierlech sinn ech Lëtzebuergerin. Natierlech means “of course”. Be aware of the word order here Natierlech sinn ech Lëtzebuergerin. As we start with natierlech you have to respect verb 2. position order. �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Nationalities When we are talking about people nationality there is a masculine and a female form. Now I said ech si Lëtzebuergerin because I am a woman. But if you are a man you would say:  Ech si Lëtzebuerger. Let’s look at some other nationalities: Ech kommen aus Italien. Ech sinn Italiener. I come from Italy. I am Italian (man).If you are a woman, say:  Ech sinn Italienerin. Ech sinn Englänner.   Ech sinn Englännerin.    I am English (man).   I am English (woman).Ech sinn Ungar.   Ech sinn Ungarin.    I am Hungarian (man).   I am Hungarian (woman).Ech si Pol  Ech si Polin.    I am Polish (man).   I am Polish (woman).Ech sinn Amerikaner Ech sinn Amerikanerin.    I am American (man).   I am American (woman).Ech si Fransous  Ech si Franséisin.    I am French (man).    I am French (woman).�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�4
Ech sinn Inder Ech sinn Inderin.    I am Indian (man).   I am Indian (woman).Ech si Spuenier Ech si Spuenierin.    I am Spanish (man).   I am Spanish (woman).Ech si Fransous  Ech si Franséisin.    I am French (man).    I am French (woman).So you notice that the female form is ending with -in. But this is not always the case. I am German is: Ech sinn Dä

LESSON NOTESWIDDERHUELUNG This is lesson 10 and it is time to go back through what you have learnt. It is a translation challenge. Listen to the video presentation of this lesson and try to translate what you hear. Translation is always good to think about the structures and also to practise your vocabulary skills. Exercise 1: From Luxembourgish into English. Conversation 1 - An der StadEntschëllegt, kënnt Dir mir soen, wou et an der Stad e Bicherbuttek gëtt?  Excuse me, can you tell me, where there is a booshop in town?  Natierlech. Et gëtt een op der Gare.  Of course. There is one at the station.A wou genee op der Gare?  And where exactly at the station? Vis-à-vis vun der Gare ass eng Apdikt. De Bicherbuttek ass nobäi.   Opposite the station is a pharmacy. The bookshop is nearby. Dir gitt ongeféier eng Minutt riichtaus an dann iwwer eng kleng Plaz.   You go approximatively one minute straight on then over a square. An de Bicherbuttek ass op der rietser Säit.  And the bookshop is on the right hand side.�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Conversation 2 - beim DokterGudde Moien. Wéi geet et Iech?  Good morning. How are you (formal)?  Oh, net esou gutt. Oh, not so well.Firwat geet et Iech net esou gutt?  Why aren’t you feeling so well? Majo, ech mengen ech hu mech erkaalt.   Well, I think, I’ve caught a cold. Deet Iech eppes wéi?   Is something causing you pain? Does something hurt you? Jo ech hu Kappwéi.  Yes, I have a headache. Hutt Dir Féiwer?   Do you have fever? Nee, ech mengen net.  No, I don’t think so.

Conversation 3 - Bei FrënnWéi gefält dir däin neit Appartement?  How do you (informal) like your new flat?  Meng Wunneng gefält mir immens gutt. Se ass um drëtte Stack an zimlech grouss. I like my flat a lot. It is on the third floor and quite big.Wéi vill Zëmmeren hues du dann?  How many rooms do you have (then)? Majo, ech hunn zwee Schlofzëmmeren, ee grousst an ee klengt.   Well, I have to bedrooms, a small one and a big one. Dann hunn ech natierlech eng Kichen, eng gemittlech Stuff an e klengt Buedzëmmer.  Then I have of course a kitchen, a cosy living room and a small bathroom. Hues du keng Garage  Don’t you have any garage?. Nee, leider net.  No, I unfortunately not.

�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�1Lesson 11 Study notes Learn to talk in the past using the perfect tense Learn to form the perfect tense by using the auxiliary verb “hunn” and the past participle
Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESWat hues du gëschter gemaach?In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn to talk in the past using the perfect tense with the auxiliary verb “hunn”.The perfect tense is a past tense. Let’s start with an example:Ech hunn de Moie geschafft  I worked / have worked this morning. Grammar - Perfect Tense  Ech hunn de Moie geschafft can be translated either to “I have worked this morning” or “I worked this morning”. The perfect tense is formed by using the auxiliary verb hunn and then we use what we call the Partizip known as “past participle”. A lot of verbs are conjugated regularly and we use the root of the verb. If we look at schaffen we have schaff which is the root - the part without -en -  and then we add in front of schaff ge- a n d  a t  t h e  e n d  o f  schaff w e  a d d  a  -t. And so schaffen becomes geschafft.     Let’s look at another example: spillen to play. The root is spill and we add ge- so gespill and then the -t at the end: gespillt. Ech hu gespillt = “I have played” or “I played”.      So this tense is used for talking about things that you have done or that you did. So there is no timeframe when we would be talking about these things. It can be this morning, it can be a hundred years ago. So in Luxembourgish it is a past tense and can be used to speak about something that happened a thousand years ago or I could talk about something that happened 1 minute ago.     �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Ech hunn zu London gewunnt.   I lived in London. Zu London comes in between hunn and gewunnt, like in our ﬁrst example ech hunn de Moie geschafft where we have as well de Moie between hunn and geschafft. The past participle comes at the very end of the sentence. Let’s now learn how to ask someone what they did yesterday. By the way “yesterday” is gëschter. So to ask someone in an informal situation “Until what time did you work yesterday?” is Bis wéi vill Auer hues du gëschter geschafft?   Until what time did you work yesterday?Ech hu gëschter bis 7 Auer owes geschafft.  I worked until 7pm yesterday.                              Wéini hues du gëschter d’Owesiesse gekacht?   When did you cook dinner yesterday?Ech hu gëschter um hallwer aacht owes gekacht.  I cooked at 7.30 pm yesterday. Or if you didn’t cook you may say Ech hu gëschter net gekacht “I didn’t cook yesterday”. Let’s look at the word order: gëschter net gekacht. By now you know that we use “time” before anything else. �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Gëschter is our ﬁrst adverb of time and net is our 2. adverb. So net is an adverb in itself. Therefore we have Ech hu gëschter net gekacht. Let’s imagine that you ask me “Anne what did you do yesterday?” This is Anne, wat

�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�1Lesson 12 Study notes Learn to use the auxiliary verb “sinn” in the perfect tense
Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESMir sinn op London geﬂunnIn this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn to talk in the past using the perfect tense with the auxiliary verb “sinn”.There is another auxiliary verb in the perfect tense. One is hunn and the other one is sinn “to be”. And you use sinn in the exact same way as hunn. For example:Ech si gëschter Owend um 10 an d’Bett gaang.  I went to bed at o’clock yesterday evening. Gaang is the past participle of goen. You can as well hear native speakers saying gaangen.  Now I hear you asking but why do we use here the verb sinn and not hunn? Let explain this with the following 3 sentences:1 Ech sinn op London geﬂunn.     I ﬂew to London.2 Ech si mam Zuch op Paräis gefuer.     I took the train to Paris.3 Meng Kanner sinn haut net an d’Schoul gaang.     My children didn’t go to school today.
�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Grammar - Perfect Tense with sinn  So the rule with the auxilary verb sinn is: use sinn when there is a change of place. All of the verbs that I chose there in the examples there is a movement or better a change of place involved. It is not just movement because we could also say that dancing or playing is movement but we say Ech hunn Tennis gespillt. So it is really when we went from A to B that we use sinn. So Saz 1: Ech sinn op London geﬂunn. Geﬂunn comes from the verb ﬂéien “to ﬂy”. Saz 2: Ech si mam Zuch op Paräis gefuer. Gefuer comes from fueren here it means “to travel”. And the last example is perhaps more obvious because we are talking about goen a n d  goen involves a movement … even if we don’t go somewhere like in my example Meng Kanner sinn haut net an d’Schoul gaang. Use sinn when you change place. Now we use sinn as well when there is a change of state: If we look at to be born Ech sinn am August gebuer. I was born in August.Here are some other verbs which express a change of state: aschlofen, erwächen, wuessen, opstoen etc Ech sinn ageschlof.   I fell asleep.As you know schlofen is “to sleep” and to say that you have slept you say ech hu geschlof. But here we are looking at aschlofen which means “to fall asleep”. There is a change of state. We are going from awake to asleep. Therefore we say Ech sinn ageschlof. And aschlofen is a separable verb. The ge comes in the middle ageschlof.�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
The opposite of falling asleep is erwächen meaning to wake up. Erwächen is not separable and in the past it becomes erwächt. Hien ass erwächt.   He woke up. Meng Duechter ass 3 cm gewuess.   My daughter grew 3 cms.Gewuess is the participle of wuessen “to grow”. Children grow Kanner wuessen but  as well plants Planze wuessen plants grow.And here another last verb expressing a change of stateThis is opstoen. Literally this means “to stand up”. And the participle is o

Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESMeng lescht VakanzIn this lesson you will learn how to talk to your friends or your family in details about your last holiday. This is a subject we often like to talk about. You will also learn useful vocabulary which you will be able to use TODAY in small talk conversation!Learn how to ask someone “What did you (informal) do on holiday?” Wat hues du an der Vakanz gemaach? What did you (informal) do on holiday? Wat hutt dir an der Vakanz gemaach? What did you (formal) do on holiday? Here are a few useful  sentences to practiseMäi Mann an ech si leschte Freideg op Lissabonn geﬂunn a sinn owes um 7 Auer ukomm. My husband and I ﬂew to Lisbon last Friday and we arrived at 7 pm.Mir sinn dräi Deeg do bliwwen. We stayed for three days there.�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
In English you use the “for” before “3 days”: for 3 days. But in Luxembourgish we just say 3 Deeg. Bliwwen is the past participle of bleiwen “to stay”. We use the auxiliary verb sinn to build the perfect tense of bleiwen. Although bleiwen does convey the idea of a position rather than a change of place we need to use sinn. This is an exception.Mir hunn e schéinen Hotel matzen am Zentrum gebucht. We booked a beautiful hotel in the middle of the town centre.Gebucht is the partizip of buchen “to book”.And we have e schéinen Hotel - schéin is an adjective and remember that in Luxembourgish adjectives, usually go in front of the noun they modify. And an adjective in front of a noun has to have an ending. Just what that ending will be, depends on the gender of the noun. As Hotel is a masculine noun we are going to add the letters -en to the adjective schéin.    Mir hunn eis Wallis ausgepaakt a sinn direkt an de Restaurant gaang. We unpacked our suitcase and we went immediately to the restaurant.Ausgepaakt is the Partizip of auspaken “to unpack” or “to empty your suitcase”. It is a separable verb and it is regular therefore we have ausgepaakt. Direkt translates here into immediately. 
�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Mir hunn immens gutt giess an e portugisesche roude Wäin dobäi gedronk.  We ate very good and to that we drank a portugese red wine.Dobäi means “to that”. Dobäi is often used when talking about food or in a restaurant, and it is not always translated into in English. Examples: Moies iessen ech oft eng Schmier an drénken en Téi dobäi.   I often eat a slice of bread in the mornings and I drink a tea (to that).Beim Menu gëtt et ëmmer eng Zopp dobäi.  With the menu there is always a soup.Let’s return to our Vakanz zu Lissabonn:Den nächsten Dag si mir fréi opgestan an hu Kafﬁ am Hotel gedronk. The next day we got up early and we had breakfast at the hotel.Grammar - Deﬁnite Articles Dative Case  I said for “at the hotel” am Hotel. In Esay Luxembourgish Level 1 y

LESSON NOTESWéi al waars du, wéi s du ….In this lesson you will learn that the verbs sinn and hunn don’t have or at least they don’t use really the  perfect tense but rather the imperfect called Preterit in Luxembourgish. And you will learn how to say “when” or “if” in Luxembourgish.Let’s start by asking someone “How was your weekend?”Wéi war däi Weekend?   How was your weekend?I’ve used war -  wéi war däi Weekend? And that is obviously of the verb sinn and in the imperfect tense. That is another past tense. Conjugating Verbssinn sinn: to be (imperfect tense)ech war mir warendu waarsdir / Dir waarthien / hatt / Si  warsi   waren
�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
hunn 
To talk further back in time -  if we know the speciﬁc time frame -  for example “a year ago” we would say virun engem Joer. Virun is comparable with “before” or “ago”. And that works for any timeframe virun 10 Joer “10 years ago”  virun 100 Joer  “100 years ago”. And you can use as well:virun engem Mount  a month ago virun enger Woch  a week ago Virun is a preposition which requires us to use the dative case. Let’s have some examples: Ech war virun enger Woch beim Coiffer.  I was to the hairdresser a week ago. Mir ware virun zwee Deeg beim neien Italiener.  We went to the new italian restaurant two days ago. hunn: to have (imperfect tense)ech hat mir hatendu hasdir / Dir hathien / hatt / Si  hatsi   haten
�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Hie war virun engem Joer vill krank.  He was very sick a year ago. But if you don’t know exactly the date when talking in the past you can either use fréier meaning “earlier” in the sense of earlier in the past or deemools more in the sense of “at that time”. You can use fréier or deemools for something that happened any time ago. For example you are talking with your family about Christmas 10 years ago and you wanted to say: “At that time I always wore black clothes”:Deemools hunn ech ëmmer schwaarz Kleeder ugedoen. At that time I always wore black clothes. Ugedoen is the past participle of undoen “to put on”.          Just to remind you:  We started with Deemols so the next element there has to be the    verb, and in our example it is the auxilary verb hunn followed by the subject ech. Deemools hunn ech  and then all the other elements come in the middle ëmmer schwaarz Kleeder and at the end we have the 2. part of our verbal part ugedoen. Let’s do a phrase with fréier. Fréier hat keen en Handy.  In the past nobody had a mobile phone. �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�4

LESSON NOTESWat hues du haut un?In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn the different clothes, the colors and how to describe a person. After this lesson you’ll be able to describe a person brieﬂy and what someone is wearing. Let’s start by learning the word for “clothes” which is Kleeder.You know already the word for “to put something on” or “to dress” which is undoen. Undoen is a separable verb. So in a phrase in the present tense you separate the preﬁx un from the root doen:  Am Summer doen ech ëmmer kuerz Boxen un.  In summer I always put on shorts.Ech hunn haut meng Kanner waarm ugedoen.  I dressed my children warmly today. And to say “I get dressed” use the verb sech undoen, for example “I get dressed at half past seven” is Ech doe mech um hallwer aacht un.And “to wear” is unhunn. This is as well a separable verb. We have the preﬁx un and the stem is the verb hunn.  So literally this means “to have on”.  Let’s do a couple of sentences with unhunn:©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved2
 Mäi Papp huet haut seng giel Krawatt un.   My father is wearing his yellow tie today. Eng giel Krawatt is “a yellow tie”. Eng Krawatt is a feminine noun. Du hues eng ﬂott Box un.  You are wearing nice trousers. Here we have the word for “a pair of trousers” which is eng Box. As you can see it is a feminine singular noun eng Box. Let’s now learn the most common clothes you need to describe a person.     My Learning Tip:    Learn the clothes straight away with their gender.       We begin with:eng Jeansbox (feminine) a pair of jeans eng kuerz Box (feminine) shorts©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved3
eng Blus (feminine) a blouseeng Jackett (feminine) a jacket / a coateng Jupe (feminine) a skirteng Mutz (feminine) a hat (woollen)en T’Shirt (masculine) a t’shirte Pullover (masculine)a pullovere Mantel (masculine) a coate Reemantel (masculine) a raincoate Short (masculine) shortse Rack (masculine) a dress©  Luxembourgish with Anne. e Kostüm (masculine) a suite Schal (masculine) a scarfen Hut (masculine) a haten Hiem (neuter)a shirtStrëmp (plural)socksSchong (plural)  shongTallekeschong (plural)  high heelsStiwwelen (plural)  bootsSandalen (plural)  Sandals

GrammarTo describe the clothes a person is wearing you need to put an adjective before the noun. My boyfriend put on today his black suit, his white

�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�1Lesson 16 Study notes Learn to express your opinion Learn how to build comparative adjectives Learn the verb “fannen”
Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESAn, wéi fënns du dës Box?In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, we will continue to talk brieﬂy about clothes and you will learn to talk about your preferences. You will learn a new verb fannen to express your opinion and how to build comparative adjectives in Luxembourgish.Let’s start with a bit of review: “I like this pair of trousers” translates into Mir gefält dës Box or you can say dës Box gefält mir. Both are correct. Literally this would be “To me pleases this  pair of trousers” but in natural English you say “I like these trousers”.And in Luxembourgish if you like to express an opinion about somebody or something you can use another verb which is fannen. It literally means “to ﬁnd” but when used to express an opinion it translates more often into “to think” or “to like”:    Ech fannen dëse Mantel ganz ﬂott.    I like this coat very much. / I think this coat is very beautifulLiterally this would be “I ﬁnd this coat very beautiful”. And here we use dëse for “this coat” because Mantel is a masculine noun dëse Mantel.Let’s practise. Imagine that you are in a shop which sells as well shoes with your partner and you wanted to ask for his / her opinion for example:�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Wéi fënns du dës wäiss Turnschlappen?  How do you like these white sneakers? Net schlecht, mee ech fannen déi schwaarz Turnschlappe méi schéin.  Not bad, but I think the black sneakers are more beautiful. So you can see that we use the verb fannen here whereas in English you are more likey to say “I think” or “I prefer”. We have méi schéin which means more beautiful.Grammar - Comparative méi schéin is a comparison. To form the comparative for most adjectives or adverbs in Luxembourgish you simply add méi meaning more. For example “more expensive” is méi deier or “cheaper” is méi bëlleg. To express relative comparisons we use the construction:          méi + adjective + wéi             “more + adjective + than”; or             grad esou + adjective + wéi   “as + adjective + as”; or            net esou + adjective + wéi      “not as + adjective + as”      Irregular comaparative forms:         gutt →bessergood / bettergär →léiwerlikeable / preferablevill→méimuch/ more
�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
A wéi fënns du déi gro Box?  And how do you like the grey pair of trousers? Net schlecht, mee ech fannen déi blo méi ﬂott wéi déi gro.  Not bad, but I think the blue ones are more beautiful than the grey ones. Mee, déi blo ass méi deier wéi déi gro.  But the blue ones are more expensive than the grey ones. Ech fannen du hues genuch Boxen.  I think that you have enough trousers.Dëse Rack ass grad esou kuerz wéi d’Jupe.  This dress is as short as the skirt.Dat schéint Hiem ass net esou bëlleg wéi den T’Shirt.

�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�1Lesson 17 Study notes Learn the word “well” which means because Learn how to use “well” in a sentence and cope with the word order  changes
Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESWell ech keng Zäit hunn.In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn how to use the word well in a sentence and cope with the word order changes.Well meaning “because” or “as” is used to give a reason for something, to justify something and this word brings with it some challenges regarding the word order. Well goes with a subordinate clause. Grammar - Subordinate Clause A subordinate clause is that part of a sentence that cannot stand alone and is dependent from the main part of the sentence. The main clause, which can stand on its own. Here is an example of a main clause: Ech ginn haut fréi an d’Bett. This means something.  We don’t need to add anything if we don’t want to. But a subordinate clause has to connect with something else. It is meaningless without the main clause. I am going to make the same sentence Ech ginn haut fréi an d’Bett. but by adding well: Ech drénke keng Taass Kafﬁ, well ech haut fréi an d’Bett ginn. “I don’t drink a cup of coffee, because I am going early to bed today”.   You see that “because I am going early to bed today” cannot stand alone. So a subordinate clause starting with well needs to be connected to the main part of the sentence.      �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Word order in a subordinate clause        The subordinate  clause is starting with well and as a result our verb is going to the end: well ech haut fréi an d’Bett ginn.            Because we have a main clause and a subordinate clause and they are separated by a comma.            D’Maria léiert Lëtzebuergesch, well säi Mann Lëtzebuerger ass. Maria is learning Luxembourgish, because her husband is Luxembourger.Den Tom huet muer keng Zäit, well hien Tennis spillt.  Tom has no time tomorrow, because he is playing Tennis.What happens with separable verbs in a subordinate clause? They are no longer separated in the subordiante clause. Example:Ech muss elo goen, well den Zuch an enger hallwer Stonn fortﬁert.  I have to go now, because the train is leaving in half an hour. So as you can see the verb fortfueren “to leave” is not separated. An enger hallwer Stonn is “in half an hour”. If you want to indicate a time difference, a span, between now and some point to come we use the preposition an + the dative case. We can as well say an enger Stonn. An is also used for a general indication of a duration that is needed for an achievement. The best way to grasp it is to think of it as the answer to “in what amount of time have you done that?” Example:�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3

�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�1Lesson 18 Study notes Learn more about the modal verb “kënnen” Learn more about the modal verb “wëllen” Learn the word order associated with these verbs 
Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESWëlls du gutt Lëtzebuergesch schwätzenIn this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn more about the modal verb kënnen and we will see another modal verb wëllen which means “to want”  and the word order rules associated with them.The modal verbs kënnen and wëllen are useful verbs you will need to ask for things and asking for people to give or bring you things or to request something. Let’s start with kënnen.Imagine that you are having lunch in a restaurant and that you wanted to ask the waiter for the bill:Kënnt Dir mir d’Rechnung bréngen, w.e.g.?  Can you bring me the bill please. PronunciationKënnt Dir is the formal form of “can you”. You write kënnt with a t at the end but that t is not really pronounced when followed by dir. Listen to the video so to tune your ears to natural spoken Luxembourgish.
�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Grammar - Word orderKënnen is a modal verb and a modal verb is always used in tandem with the inﬁnitive form of another verb,   so we have 2 verbs in our sentence: kënnen and bréngen. And as we start with the modal verb (kënnen)  we take the main verb (bréngen) to the end of the sentence. Kënnt D i r  m i r  d ’ R e c h n u n g  bréngen, w.e.g. You can as well put the w.e.g after mir: Kënnt Dir mir w.e.g. d’Rechnung bréngen.             This is also correct.       Or if you would like the waiter to bring you the salt you can ask:Kënnt Dir mir d’Salz bréngen, w.e.g.?  Can you bring me the salt, please?Kënnt Dir mir w.e.g. d’Salz bréngen?  Can you bring me the salt, please?D’Salz is the salt and it is a neuter noun and pepper is Peffer and it is masculine. Kënnt Dir mir de Peffer bréngen, w.e.g.? Can you bring me the pepper, please?Now let’s imagine that you are sitting around the table with friends, so in an informal situation, then you would not ask Kënnt Dir but you would rather use the du-form and this is  kanns du and then rather than asking “can you bring me the salt” you would say “can you give me the salt, please?” The word for give is ginn.�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Kanns du mir d’Salz ginn, w.e.g.?  Can you give me the salt, please?Kanns du mir w.e.g. d’Salz ginn?  Can you give me the salt, please?There is another verb which is commonly used in informal situations to ask someone to pass you something and this is the verb kréien. It literally means to get or to receive. Example to ask “Can I have / get the water please?” you would ask Kann ech d’Waasser kréien, w.e.g.? And then you use Kann ech “can I”. In a coffee shop you would hear native speakers asking:   Kann ech e Kafﬁ kréien, w.e.g.? Can I have / get a coffee please?Wëllen  is as well a modal verb and can be used in the same way as kënnen but more in

�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�1Lesson 19 Study notes Learn what a reﬂexive verb is Learn to use reﬂexive verbs 
Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESEch freeë mech op d’VakanzIn this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn to use reﬂexive verbs in Luxembourgish. In addition to being able to talk about “washing yourself” you will also learn to talk about “looking forward to things”, “relaxing”, “catching a cold”, “remembering things”, etc …What is a reﬂexive verb? A reﬂexive verb describes an action of someone (subject) where the action reﬂects back to the subject. In other words, whoever is speaking is doing an action to himself. For example sech wäschen “to wash yourself”, sech raséieren “to shave yourself”, sech undoen. Luxembourgish reﬂexive verbs are used in connection with a reﬂexive pronoun.Conjugating Verbssech wäschen is a reﬂexive but regular verbThe participle is: gewäschsech wäschen: to wash oneself (present tense)ech                    wäsche mechmir                 wäsche eis / onsdu                      wäschs dechdir / Dir           wäscht iechhien/hatt/Si        wäscht sechsi                    wäsche sech�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
A reﬂexive pronoun is an object pronoun. For example: mech (myself), dech (yourself), sech (himself), eis (ourselves), iech (yourselves) … Use as well Iech for the formal version and  themselves is sech. Let’s make a sentence:Ech wäsche mech moies um 7 Auer.                                                  I wash myself at 7amSech freeën op / iwwerBut there are many verbs where we don’t have really a reason why it would be reﬂexive. So there is no SELF element to it and we don’t really translate in a reﬂexive way in English such as “to look forward to something” and that is sech freeën. So how can you know if a verb is reﬂexive or not? You will only know it by looking it up in the dictionary. Let’s make a sentence:Ech freeë mech op dës Lektioun. I am looking forward to this lesson. “I Iook forward to” is ech freeë mech and as you can see it is  reﬂexive in Luxembourgish. Now when you are looking up freeën in the dictionary it would say reﬂexive so you know that you have to use the reﬂexive pronoun sech.  �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Conjugating Verbssech freeën is a reﬂexive but regular verb
The participle is: gefreetIn English we have “I look forward to something” and in Luxembourgish we need a preposition with sech freeën. The preposition is op -  sech freeën op and op is what we would translate as “to” in English. And as we have a preposition we have to think which case to use. And here we use the accusative case: Ech freeë mech op dës Lektioun. But be carefull: we could as well say Ech freeë mech iwwer dës Lektioun which means something a bit different, more in the sense “I am excited about our lesson” so I’m not looking forward to it but it’s already now, we are about to talk about it now. And we use as well the

©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved1Lesson Study notesLearn how to say what you like and what you don’t likeLearn how to use the verb “gefalen”Learn the personal pronouns 
Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESWéi gefält dir Lëtzebuerg?In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish - Level 2, you’ll learn how to say what you like and what you don’t like and the correct use of the verbs gefalen and scmaachen.In the lessons 32 and 33 of Easy Luxembourgish - Level 1 you have learnt what you like doing and what you don’t like doing. Remember to use the following construction:Verb + gär / net gärMéchs du gär Sport? Do you like to do sports?Jo, ech maache gär Sport. Yes, I like to do sports.Gitt Dir gär an de Kino? Do you like going to the cinema?Nee, ech gi guer net gär an de Kino. No, I don’t like at all to go to the cinema. ©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved2
Let’s move on with today’s lesson and learn to say that you like something for example “I like my job”. This is Mir gefält meng Aarbecht.  I like my job. Literally this would be: “To me pleases my job”. But in better English you say “I like my job”. Mir gefält meng Aarbecht. Or you can as well say Meng Aarbecht gefält mir. “My work pleases to me”. You can use the same construction for other things: Mir gefält de Film.  I like the movie. You can use the same construction to say that you like someone: Mir gefält mäin Noper.  I like my neighbour. Here are some phrases that you may ﬁnd useful when chatting to a new friendLëtzebuerg gefält mir.  I like Luxembourg. Lëtzebuerg gefält mir ganz gutt.  I like Luxembourg very much. 
©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved3
Mir gefält deng Wunneng. I like your ﬂat. Mir gefält deng Wunneng ganz gutt. I like your ﬂat very much. Mir gefält däi Rack. I like your dress. D’Wieder gefält mir net. I don’t like the weather. D’Wieder gefält mir guer net zu Lëtzebuerg. I don’t like at all the weather in Luxembourg. GrammarI am going to go over this new verb which is gefalen. As you might have guessed from the phrase mir gefält, gefalen is a verb that requires us to use the dative case. You recognise immediately the word mir, which we learnt means something like “to me”. Let’s look at our example of:                                                                                                         Mir gefält meng Aarbecht. Literally this means “to me pleases my work” or “my work pleases me”. Again, we have the idea that this dative form of the personal pronoun in this case mir means “to me”. Even though the “to” disappears when we change the word order in English. When comparing it to the English you can also see how mir is the object and meng Aarbecht is the subject. As mir is the thing “being pleased” and meng Aarbecht is the thing “doing the pleasing”. That’s why meng Aarbecht stays in the nominative case. Now this probably all sounds a little bit more complicated that it needs to be.    ©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All R

Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESEch hu mech gutt entspaantIn this last lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn to use reﬂexive verbs in the past. You will learn to say what you enjoyed, what you looked forward to, what you complained about etc. Also ech hoffen, du frees dech iwwer d’Lektioun vun haut.  Well I hope that you are excited about today’s lesson.Because we will deal with reﬂexive verbs in the past. Do you remember how you build the perfect tense? And hopefully your answer is: Jo, ech erënnere mech.  Yes I remember. To build the perfect tense we use either the auxiliary verb hunn or sinn and a verb in the past participle. And now I have some good news: when we are looking at the reﬂexive verbs in the perfect tense in Luxembourgish we only use hunn. Let’s do a sentence and put it from the present into the perfect tense:D’Eltere këmmere sech ëm hir Kanner.  The parents take care of their children. �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Our verb is sech këmmeren ëm “to take care of” and the Partizip is gekëmmert.D’Elteren hu sech ëm hir Kanner gekëmmert. The parents have taken care or took care of their children. Let’s do a few more sentences. Ech treffe mech mam Sarah.  I meet up with Sarah.Here the verb is sech treffen the Partizip is getraff. Now the sentence in the perfect tense is: Ech hu mech mam Sarah getraff.   I met up with Sarah.And we could add where “I met up with Sarah” for example: Ech hu mech virum Kino mam Sarah getraff. And if we wanted to add at what time for example “ten o’clock” we would put the time before anything else:Ech hu mech um 10 Auer virum Kino mam Sarah getraff.   I meet up with Sarah before the cinema at 10 o’clock.Den Henri iergert sech iwwer de Kaméidi. Henri gets annoyed about the noise.�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Sech iergeren is a regular verb so the Partizip is geiergert. Let’s add WHEN Henri got annoyed for example yesterday evening:Den Henri huet sech gëschter Owend iwwer de Kaméidi geiergert.  Henri got annoyed about the noise yesterday evening.And if wanted to add “a lot” the sentence would be:Den Henri huet sech gëschter Owend vill iwwer de Kaméidi geiergert.  Henri got annoyed a lot about the noise yesterday evening.Vill means “a lot” and it is an adverb and it comes in the middle of our phrase. But there is also the possibility to take an adverb out of the middle and to put it at the beginning of the sentence for example a time adverb such as gëschter Owend. We then put emphasize on the time aspect. So we can start with Gëschter Owend … Gëschter Owend huet den Henri sech vill iwwer de Kaméidi geiergert.   Henri got annoyed a lot about the noise yesterday evening. When starting with an adverb you should remember to put your verb “second element” followed by the subject.Um 10 Auer hunn ech mech virum Kino mam Sarah ge

LESSON NOTESWou deet et Iech wéi?In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish - Level 2, you’ll learn various parts of the body, and how to explain your pains and aches and the verb wéidoen.Let’s start with some possible answers to the question Wéi geet et dir / Iech? Mir geet et gutt / ganz gutt. I’m feeling well / very well.Alles an der Rei. Everything is in order / okMir geet et net esou gutt. I’m not feeling so well.Mir geet et guer net gutt. I’m not feeling well at all.
�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
And if you are really feeling sick you may say et ass mir schlecht. Literally this means “it to me is bad” but in correct English this translates to I’m feeling bad / sick.Ech si krank. I am sick. Now let’s  follow up and reply to mir ass et schlecht with the question “Why?” Firwat? You could say:Firwat geet et dir net esou gutt?  Why aren’t you feeling so good?Remember that dir in this phrase is the informal way to say  “to you” in Luxembourgish. The formal form of “to you” is Iech. If you don’t remember when to say dir, du, iech go back to lesson 2 and 3 of Easy Luxembourgish Level 1.Here are some possible answers to that question:Ech hu Kappwéi.  I have headache / a sore head Ech hu Bauchwéi.  I have stomachache.Ech hu Réckwéi.  I have backache.�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Ech hunn Halswéi.  I have throatache.Ech hunn Zännwéi.  I have toothache.In English when  you have a part of the body that doesn’t normally have a word with “-ache” after it, so for example “hand” you say “My hand is sore” or “I have a pain in my hand”. In Luxembourgish this is Meng Hand deet mir wéi. So Hand is “hand” and as it is a feminine noun you say meng Hand. And deet mir wéi literally means “does to me hurt”. Deet wéi is “does hurt” and the verb is wéidoen meaning  “to hurt” or “to cause pain”. Meng Hand deet mir wéi. My hand does hurt.Conjugating Verbswéidoen is an irregular verb

All Rights reserved�4

Mäin Aarm deet mer wéi.   My arm does hurt.Mäi Fouss deet mer wéi.   My foot hurts.Mäi Been deet mer wéi.   My leg hurts.Now to say “my legs are sore” you have to use the plural of Been which is as well Been so exact the same and then we’ve got:Meng Been di mer wéi. And to say “I have a cough” we use the deﬁnite masculine article den.Ech hunn de Schnapp.  I have a runny nose. Ech hunn eng Erkältung I have a cold.And here we use like in English the indeﬁnite article eng. You will often hear native speaker saying:�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2

So when you tell someone to take dëst Medikament you are actually telling them which medicine

LESSON NOTESGëtt et hei eng Schwämm?In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn to ask “Is there … ?” and some possible answers to that question and you will learn to understand the location of places. And what do you need to be able to describe the position of an object or a place ….. prepositions!Let’s learn how to ask something “…does exist in a town…?” for example:Gëtt et hei eng Bibliothéik?  Is there a library here?Gëtt et hei eng Bäckerei?  Is there a bakery here?Gëtt et hei en Touristebüro?  Is there a tourist ofﬁce here?Gëtt et hei …..? simply translates into “is there here ….?” in the sense of “does somthing exist …?” If you wanted to ask someone if something is “close by” in the sense of “in the neighbourhood” use nobäi�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Gëtt et e Musée nobäi? Is there a museum nearby?Gëtt et eng Post nobäi? Is there a post ofﬁce nearby?And a possible answer could be: Jo, et gëtt eng Post nëmmen 5 Minutten vun hei.   There is a post ofﬁce only 5 minutes from here. Nëmmen 5 Minutte vun hei means “only 5 minutes from here”. Or you may hear:Jo, d’Post as niewent der Schoul.   Yes the post office is beside the school. Grammar - Dative case“Beside” is niewent and “beside the school” is niewent der Schoul.    Now you have learnt that “the school” is d’Schoul. But when stating that something is beside another place -   in our example d’Schoul - you have to use the dative case and as d’Schoul is a feminine noun it changes to der Schoul in the dative case. If you are not yet familiar with the dative case I recommend that you listen to lesson 25 of Easy Luxembourgish 1 where I introduced you to the dative case in details. The dative case can either be an object or an description where something or someone is. So the deﬁnite article in the dative case is der for feminine nouns and dem for masculine and neuter nouns.    �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Let’s take an example with a masculine noun: de Kino Et gëtt e Park niewent dem Kino.There is a park beside the cinema. De Kino becomes in the dative case dem Kino. It is as well dem ﬁr neuter nouns:Et gëtt e Park niewent dem Spidol. There is a park beside the hospital. Grammar - PrepositionsI said that the dative case is used to describe where someone is or the position of a certain object. And what do we need to be able to describe the position of an object? ….. Prepositions. There are prepositions that require always the dative case for example mat: “with you” is mat dir and there are prepositions which always require the accusative case such as ﬁr meaning for: “for you” is ﬁr dech.           And there are the so called two way prepositions like niewent which requires us to use either the accus

LESSON NOTESWéini sinn d’Butteker op?In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn useful words and phrases to ask for information and understand possible answers to your questions. And you will learn to use the relative pronouns - more precisely - a translation of the word “which”.To ask in general “When is something opened?” for example “When does the bakery open?” you will say  Wéini ass d’Bäckerei op?   When does the bakery open? Wéini ass d’Bäckerei zou?   When does the bakery close? So “open” is op. And “closed “ is zou. Both op and zou come to the end of a sentence. A possible answer to the question Wéini ass de Supermarché op? could be De Supermarché ass vu méindes bis samschdes op.  The supermarket is open from Monday to Saturday.Remember to say here méindes and samschdes as the supermarché is always open on these days. And “from … to” is vun … bis.�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Or other possible answers could beDe Supermarché ass all Dag op, ausser sonndes mëttes. The supermarket is open everyday except on Sundays afternoon. De Supermarché ass vu méindes bis samschdes vun 8 Auer moies bis 7 Auer owes op. The supermarket is open from monday to saturday from 8 am until 7 pm. Ausser means “except”. And “on sundays afternoons” is simply sonndes mëttes. D’Butteker si sonndes zou. The shops are closed on SundaysThe plural form of de Buttek “the shop” is d’Butteker “the shops”.Let’s learn some more phrases:D’Schwämm mécht all Dag um 9h30 op, ausser freides moies.  The swimming pool opens every day at 9.30 am except on Fridays morning.Méindes moies ass d’Apdikt zou, mee nomëttes ass se vun 13h15bis 18h00 op.On Monday morning the pharmacy is closed, but in the afternoon it is openfrom 1.15pm to 6pm.Gëtt et hei e Supermarché, dee(n) sonndes op ass? Is there here a supermarket which is open on Sundays?    �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Deen which translates into “which” introduces a so-calledsubordinate phrase and in a subordinate clause the verb comes atthe very end of the phrase.And a possible answer to that question could beJo, et gëtt een op der Gare. Mee, en ass nëmme moies op.  Yes, there is one at the station. But it is only open in the mornings.Grammar - Personal PronounsNow we have here this small word en in the previous sentence we had se. En and se are personal pronouns referring to thing(s) spoken about. Pronouns are simply small words which replace nouns.Whereas in English you would say 'it', in Luxembourgish it could be en or se or et, depending on the gender of the noun. Let’s take the example of our phrases:                                                                                                     D’Apdikt a

LESSON NOTESFir d’éischt ginn ech …In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn to describe your day in town to someone with more details using natural language.Let’s imagine that it is Saturday and that you decide to go to town. And you tell your partner what you will do and where you will go. I recommend that you listen to the video presentation of this lesson•to understand my explanations•to practise your listening comprehension skills, and•to practise your pronunciation.Dat ass mäi Programm:Sou, ech fueren elo an d’Stad. Fir d’éischt ginn ech gemittlech op de Maart Geméis, Kéis a Blumme kafen. Dono ginn ech an d’Apdikt meng Mediakamenter sichen. An dann hunn ech um eng Auer RV mam Annick op der Plëss. Mir ginn zesummen bei den Inder iessen, deen niewent dem Palais ass. Nom Iessen gi mir nach e bëssen an d’Stad trëppelen. An éier ech heemkommen, ginn ech nach séier an de Supermarché. Brauchs de eppes?

Grammar - Adverbs  Duerno (dono, herno), dann, endlech are adverbs. If you start your          sentence with an adverb or with ﬁr d’éischt you must follow the “Verb    2. position rule”.                                                                                          Examples:                                                                                                  Dono ginn ech nach akafen.                                                                        Fir d’éischt ginn ech mat Frënn iessen an dann fueren ech heem.                                         Conjugating Verbssichen is a regualr verbThe participle is: gesicht. “Did you search your glasses? ” is Hues du däi Brëll gesicht?brauchen is an irregualr verb which conjugates like a modal verbThe participle is: gebraucht. “Did you need an umbrella? ” is Hues du e Prabbeli gebraucht?sichen: to look for / to search (present tense)ech

�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�1Lesson 9 Study notes Learn useful vocabulary to describe your home Learn adjective endings Practise with a natural small talk conversation Learn the verbs wëssen (to know) and mengen (to think)
Easy Luxembourgish  Level 2
Lesson notes
LESSON NOTESMir sichen e klengt Haus ….In this lesson of Easy Luxembourgish, you will learn to describe your ﬂat or house to a new friend by using adjectives and you will practise the new vocablulary with a small talk conversation. And you will learn the verbs wëssen and mengen.Let’s imagine a situation where you are at friends, so in an informal situation and you are talking about your home. To ask someone “where do you live?” is  Wou wunns du? or in case you are talking to a couple you would ask Wou wunnt Dir? And your answer isEch wunnen an engem Haus zu Mamer.  I live in a house in Mamer. “House” is Haus and it is a neuter noun. “The house” is d’Haus and “e house” is en Haus. As we have the preposition an and as we are revealing the position of our house we use the dative case: Ech wunnen an engem Haus zu Mamer. “A ﬂat” is en Appartement (neuter) or eng Wunneng (feminine).Mäi Mann an ech wunnen an engem Appartement an der Stad.  My husband and I live in a ﬂat in Lux city. Mäi Mann an ech wunnen an enger Wunneng an der Stad.  My husband and I live in a ﬂat in Lux city. �©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�2
Learn to describe certain aspects of your home by placing the adjective before the noun. And this with sentences in the nominative or accusative case. In a conversation, you will be likely to sayMir hunn e grousst Haus.  We have a big house. Mir hunn e klengt Appartement.  We have a small flat. “Big” is grouss, “small” is kleng and Haus is neuter. So “a big house” becomes e grousst Haus. As Appartement is as well neuter you could also say e klengt Appartement.Mir hunn e grousst Iesszëmmer.  We have a big dining room. Mir hunn e klengt Buedzëmmer.  We have a small bathroom. Um éischte Stack hu mir e klengt Schlofzëmmer.  On the first floor we have a small bedroom. Now all these words Zëmmer, Iesszëmmer, Schlofzëmmer and Buedzëmmer are neuter nouns. 
�©  Luxembourgish with Anne. All Rights reserved�3
Grammar - Adjective endings  Grouss and kleng are adjectives and when an adjective comes before a noun the ending changes. Here is the rule in the nominative case and accusative case:                                                If the noun is neuter such as Zëmmer we add -t to the adjective. For example: e grousst Haus, en neit Zëmmer.          If the noun is masculine such as Keller we add-en to the adjective. For example: e grousse(n)  Keller, e klenge(n) Gaart.         If the noun is feminine or plural such as the adjective ending doesn’t change. For example: Eng nei Kichen, grouss Zëmmeren.                                       Ech hunn eng kleng awer gemittlech Kichen.   I have a small but cosy kitchen.Ech hunn zwee nei Schlofzëmmeren.   I have
